title: Correcting a Faulty Sieve of Eratosthenes

statement.markdown: Given a range of integers from 1 to `n`, the Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm is used to find all prime numbers in that range. However, the current implementation contains a logical error. Your task is to identify and fix the bug to obtain the correct prime numbers.
statement.inputFormat: Your function should take a single integer `n` as input, representing the upper limit of the range.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return a boolean array of size `n + 1`, where `true` indicates a prime number and `false` indicates a non-prime number.
statement.notes: The Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm uses a boolean array `isPrime` to keep track of whether each number is prime or not. Initialize all numbers as `true` (prime) and then mark the multiples of each prime number starting from 2.

examples.output: [true, true, false, true, false, true, false, true, true, false]
examples.explanation: The prime numbers in the range 1 to 10 are 2, 3, 5, 7.

hints.text: Pay attention to the initialization of the `isPrime` array.
The algorithm has a logical flaw in the way it marks multiples of each prime number.

editorial.title: Correcting the Sieve of Eratosthenes Algorithm
editorial.intuition: The Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm uses a boolean array to keep track of whether each number is prime or not. The key is to initialize all numbers as `true` and then mark the multiples of each prime number starting from 2.
editorial.bruteForce: One approach is to use a brute-force algorithm to check each number for primality by checking divisibility up to the square root of the number.
editorial.betterSolution: The correct Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm can be implemented by iterating through the `isPrime` array, marking the multiples of each prime number starting from 2.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a boolean array to keep track of prime numbers, where `true` indicates a prime number and `false` indicates a non-prime number.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the algorithm can be proven by showing that it correctly marks prime numbers and non-prime numbers in the `isPrime` array.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm should be performed to identify the logical error and correct it.
